entries.finding: Tempo now ≈2:1 ✓ (was ~1:1 — the One-Two drill is landing). Path straight (SBST) on BOTH putters — matches Journey Session 4. Face square through impact, no visible toe-over. Balls start dead on the center line. Early lift can't be judged from overhead.

entries.setup: Baseline re-analysis · batch 1 of old clips · 1 overhead + 4 ground-level DTL · Phantom 7.5
entries.finding: EARLY LIFT confirmed on film: head rises steeply off the mat in the backstroke and hangs visibly high after impact — the 'up & out' before-picture is now documented. Old backswing ≈0.40s vs ≈0.65s in the new clips: a ~60% longer, slower load. Path was straight even then. Note: frame counts read the old tempo nearer 2:1 than the logged ~1:1 — the measurable change is the longer load, not the ratio.

entries.setup: Baseline re-analysis · all 10 v1/v2 clips · overhead + ground-level DTL + side views · Phantom 7.5
entries.finding: EARLY LIFT fully documented from three angles: steep rise in the backstroke, head hanging high after impact — in the side view it exits the frame upward. Old backswing ≈0.40s vs ≈0.65s in Session 4: a ~60% longer load. Path straight and starts on-line in every old overhead — the stroke's bones were always good. Face-closing was too subtle to confirm or refute at analysis frame rate. This is the yardstick your next face-on film gets measured against.

entries.text: BUY (decision locked): zero-torque at 34", lie set flat — Mezz.1 Max → Odyssey S2S → Spider ZT. Pick with the 10-ball 5-footer test at the shop

detail.story: Frame-extracted at 15fps and read frame-by-frame. The backswing now runs about 0.65 seconds against a 0.30-second downstroke — right at the 2:1 target, and a visibly longer, calmer load than the baseline. The head tracked the mat lines straight back and through on the Phantom and the Newport 2 alike, the wings stayed symmetric to the line through the hitting zone (no visible toe-over), and every ball started dead on the center line.
detail.compare: Versus the baseline clips: backswing load grew from ≈0.40s to ≈0.65s (~60% longer). The face-closing logged in Session 1 did not appear on any rep. The left miss did not appear on camera.
detail.limits: Overhead cannot see vertical head movement, so the early-lift fix is still unverified — that is what the face-on film request is for. Small face rotations can hide inside motion blur at 15fps.

detail.story: All ten v1/v2 clips re-analyzed with the same frame-extraction method as Session 4, giving measured numbers on both ends of the comparison. The early lift is documented from three separate angles: the head rises steeply off the mat during the short backstroke, passes low at impact, then climbs immediately — in the side view it exits the top of the frame on the follow-through. The backswing measured ≈0.40s: short, steep and brisk.
detail.compare: One honest disagreement with the journey: frame counts read the old back-to-through ratio nearer 2:1 than the logged ~1:1. What actually screamed 'quick and hit-y' was the short, steep stroke overall — the measured improvement is the longer, lower, more deliberate load, not the ratio.
detail.limits: No true face-on clip exists in the archive, so lift height couldn't be measured in inches — the side view is the best available yardstick until the next face-on session.

entries.setup: 5 clips · eye-level overhead · L.A.B. DF3i — first filmed reps
entries.finding: Start line: dead on the center line, all 5 reps — no left pull anywhere. Tempo measured 0.55s back / 0.23s down ≈ 2.4:1 (motion analysis, steadiest clip). The stroke changes are holding with the new putter in the bag.

metrics.n: tracked the dashed line every rep

detail.story: First filmed strokes with the DF3i, shot from an eye-level overhead with your feet in frame. Every one of the five balls started dead on the mat's center line and tracked it until it left the frame — the miss the whole project was built around simply did not appear. Tempo was measured objectively on the steadiest clip using frame-to-frame motion analysis: quiet address, takeaway, a clear transition dip, and an impact spike gave 0.55s back and 0.23s down — about 2.4:1, squarely in the tour window. The follow-through consistently finishes with the head over the line and holds.
detail.compare: Versus Session 4 (Phantom): tempo holding at ~2:1+ (0.55s vs 0.65s load — slightly brisker but the ratio is intact). Versus baseline: a different stroke entirely. The zero-torque head now guarantees the square face that used to depend on timing.
detail.limits: Four of five clips were handheld with visible sway, which limited motion analysis to the steadiest clip. This angle still can't see vertical lift — the face-on film remains the last open item. Next session: prop the phone against something instead of holding it.

briefing.focus: Fairway-finder day. 139 slope, narrow and tree-lined — the mini driver and 2-iron are your scoring clubs off the tee, and bogey is never an emergency here.

sections.t: The course in one paragraph
sections.b: Mungeam design (2004) carved into a hillside: 6,935 yards, par 71, rating 73.7 / slope 139 from the tips — the longest, toughest, best-kept muni in Westchester. Narrow fairways lined with trees and fescue, elevated tees AND elevated greens, a few blind shots, and almost no flat lies. Front nine is an accuracy exam; back nine wants length and short game. Play the tees that match a 6,300–6,500 game, not the tips — the slope is real.

sections.t: Off the tee — your bag is built for this place
sections.b: This is exactly why you carry the r7 Mini and the 2-iron. Blind or squeezed landing area (the 2nd is famously tight, the 7th is a beast): mini driver (~220) or 2-iron (~205) and walk to the fairway. Save the Stealth 2 for the open, longer back-nine holes where the extra 15 yards matters. One rule all day: on any tee shot you can't fully see, take the club you'd bet $100 finds short grass.

sections.t: The lies are the hidden test
sections.b: Hillside course = sidehill everything. Ball above your feet wants to go LEFT — with your strong lofts, take one less club and aim right edge. Ball below feet pushes right; widen the stance and swing smooth. Uphill approach: club UP, always — elevated greens reject anything short and roll it 20 yards back down the hill. Short-siding yourself here is the double-bogey machine; center of every green is a genuinely good shot.

sections.t: Wedges & lay-ups
sections.b: When you punch out or lay up, do it to YOUR numbers: 95 (full 56°) or 108 (full 50°) — not 'as far as possible' into an awkward half-shot from a sloped lie. On uphill wedges into elevated greens, the ball lands steep and stops fast: fly it to the flag.

sections.t: Greens — first real test for the DF3i
sections.b: Reviewers call them tour-fast. Perfect debut for the zero-torque: your job is speed only, the face is handled. Lag ladder mindset from 25+ feet — die everything into the 6-foot circle. Spend the first three greens calibrating pace, and on breaking putts play extra break with dying speed. Keep the low-through-impact feel from the gate drill: that is still your one open fault, and fast greens punish a popped stroke worst.

sections.t: Logistics & scoring mindset
sections.b: Extremely hilly — take the cart, save the legs for the back nine. July heat means carries play a touch longer (load the weather tile at the first tee — the ladder's Today column does the math). Target: doubles are the enemy, not bogeys. Count them; fewer than three doubles on a 139 slope is a won day. Log the round in Coach after — score, putts, and what bit you — and I'll debrief it against this plan.

entries.setup: FULL SWING · 2 clips · simulator bay · screen-recorded
entries.finding: First long-game film on record: full coiled shoulder turn, unhurried ~3:1 tempo, and a balanced held finish — with the visible tracer running straight up the middle. No sign of the putting lift habit leaking into the full swing.

metrics.n: back to target at the top

detail.story: Two simulator-bay swings, captured as screen-recorded story clips. The good stuff is structural and it's all there: a genuinely full shoulder coil at the top (not an arms lift), an unhurried transition — the backswing takes roughly three times the downswing, which is right where a full swing should live — and a committed, balanced finish that holds while the ball flies. The one readable tracer runs straight up the middle of the fairway. Notably, the up-and-out habit from the putting baseline does not appear here: the follow-through wraps full and low.
detail.compare: First full-swing entry, so this IS the baseline. The putting project's faults look putting-specific — encouraging for the long game.
detail.limits: Screen-recorded social clips: heavy compression, UI overlays, and one camera angle per swing. Club face, path, plane, and strike location can't be judged from this. For a real swing breakdown, film direct slo-mo (240fps) — down-the-line at hand height and face-on — and send the raw clips.

entries.text: For a real swing breakdown: film 3 swings slo-mo, down-the-line at hand height + face-on, raw clips (not screen recordings)

briefing.focus: Conventional vs. left-hand-low vs. claw — decided by makes and miss direction, not feel. One stage at a time.

sections.t: Why your right hand is the suspect
sections.b: For a right-handed putter, the trail hand is the strong, dominant one — under pressure it wants to rotate and flip the face closed through impact, and a closed face is a pull left. That is literally what Session 1's frame-by-frame showed: toe-over rotation, varying rep to rep. Both alternative grips exist to take that rotation out of the stroke. The DF3i already removes most of the need — this experiment measures what's left.

sections.t: The three contenders
sections.b: CONVENTIONAL (reverse overlap): handle in the palms, palms parallel to the face, thumbs down the Press Pistol's flat front, pressure 3/10, right palm 'along for the ride.' LEFT-HAND-LOW: lead hand below trail — levels the shoulders and turns the stroke into a shoulder-rocked pendulum; the structural fix, and a natural pair with a straight SBST stroke. CLAW: normal left hand, right hand in a pencil/saw hold — the surgical 'delete the right hand' move; smallest change, shines inside 10 feet.

sections.t: Stage 0 — baseline the conventional grip FIRST (weeks 1–2)
sections.b: You just changed the two biggest levers (zero-torque head, lie). Roll the DF3i with your normal grip for two weeks: one 20-ball 5-footer test per week, tapped into the Putting Lab with miss directions. GATE: 2 or fewer left misses per 20 in both weeks → conventional wins, experiment over, don't add complexity to a solved problem. 3+ lefts in either week → advance to Stage 1.

sections.t: Stage 1 — the head-to-head (only if the gate trips)
sections.b: Practice-green session: alternate 10-ball blocks from 5 feet — conventional, LHL, claw, then repeat (60 balls, two blocks each). Log every block in the Decisions 10-ball tester named 'DF3i · conventional', 'DF3i · LHL', 'DF3i · claw' with miss pattern notes. Then add the lag check: 5 balls from 30 feet with each grip — average leave distance matters as much as makes, because alternative grips usually tax distance feel first. Two separate sessions minimum before any verdict.

sections.t: Stage 2 — commit and groove
sections.b: Winner = most makes + fewest left misses across all blocks, with a lag leave you can live with. Commit to it for 3 full weeks before re-judging (every grip change feels bad for two weeks — that's adaptation, not information). Hybrid option is legitimate: conventional for lag, claw inside 10 feet, if the blocks split that way. Film one face-on session with the winner so the early-lift verdict lands on the final setup.

sections.t: Hardware note
sections.b: The Press Pistol 2° stays — flat-front, face-square-friendly, and its built-in forward press works for all three holds. The only future nudge if hands stay noisy: an oversized SuperStroke-style grip, which damps wrist rotation further. Later, only if the data asks for it.

briefing.focus: Conventional vs. left-hand-low vs. claw — decided by makes and miss direction, not feel. Full hold instructions + setup constants inside.

sections.t: The setup constants — same for all three grips
sections.b: These never change during the experiment, so the grip is the only variable. BALL POSITION: one to two ball-widths inside your lead (left) heel — forward of center, so the DF3i meets the ball just past the low point with a slight upstroke for true roll (ball too far back = downward strike = the hop-and-skid your old thin contact hinted at). EYES: directly over the ball or a hair inside the line — run the nose-drop test once to set it (drop a ball from the bridge of your nose at address; it should land on the ball or just inside). STANCE: feet shoulder-width, knees soft, weight 55/45 favoring the lead side, and dead still — the stroke moves, the body doesn't. DISTANCE FROM BALL: arms hang straight down from the shoulders, no reaching — this also keeps the sole flat (the toe-up aim-left problem lives here). ALIGNMENT: square the face to your line FIRST, then build the stance around the face. PRESS: none added — the Press Pistol 2° has the forward lean built in; hands sit even with the ball.

sections.t: Grip 1 · Conventional (reverse overlap) — the exact hold
sections.b: LEFT HAND first, at the top: the handle runs up your LIFELINE (the crease between thumb pad and heel pad), NOT across the fingers like a full-swing grip — this locks the wrist. Left thumb flat, straight down the Press Pistol's flat front. RIGHT HAND below it: same palm placement — handle up the lifeline, right thumb flat down the front — so both PALMS FACE EACH OTHER, parallel to the putter face. They now work as one unit: if the palms oppose, the face can't rotate without both wrists agreeing. FINISH: lift your left index finger and lay it OVER the fingers of the right hand (across the pinky and ring finger) — that's the reverse overlap, and it further handcuffs the right wrist. Pressure 3/10, both hands. Feel: the back of the left hand IS the putter face — swing it at the target; the right palm just mirrors.

sections.t: Grip 2 · Left-hand-low — the exact hold
sections.b: Everything mirrors: RIGHT HAND goes at the TOP now — handle up the right lifeline, right thumb flat down the front. LEFT HAND below it — handle up the left lifeline, left thumb down the front, palms opposing as always. OVERLAP: lay your right index finger over the fingers of the left hand (the mirror of reverse overlap). CRITICAL CHECKS: your shoulders should feel — and be — LEVEL at address (that's the whole point; the normal grip tilts the right shoulder down), and your LEFT wrist stays flat and leads the stroke like a paddle; the left hand going low turns your lead side into the engine and demotes the right hand to a passenger. Expect it to feel alien for two weeks and expect lag to suffer first — that's why the 30-foot check is in the protocol.

sections.t: Grip 3 · Claw — the exact hold
sections.b: LEFT HAND: identical to conventional — top of the grip, up the lifeline, thumb down the front. This hand now does ALL the holding. RIGHT HAND: rotate it so the palm faces YOUR BODY (not the target), then hold the grip like a PENCIL — thumb pad resting on the back of the handle, the first two fingertips lying on the front/top. The handle sits in the notch between thumb and index finger. Pressure 2/10 — the right hand is a fairing, not a motor; it physically cannot rotate the face from that position, which is the entire trick. Right elbow drifts slightly away from your side — let it. Variation if the pencil feels twitchy: the SAW — same idea but all four right fingertips lie across the front of the grip like holding a saw handle. Either counts as 'claw' in the blocks; pick one and keep it.

sections.t: Why the right hand is the suspect
sections.b: For a right-handed putter, the trail hand is the strong, dominant one — under pressure it wants to rotate and flip the face closed through impact, and a closed face is a pull left. That is literally what Session 1's frame-by-frame showed: toe-over rotation, varying rep to rep. LHL and claw both exist to take that rotation out of the stroke. The DF3i already removes most of the need — this experiment measures what's left.

sections.t: Stage 0 — baseline the conventional grip FIRST (weeks 1–2)
sections.b: You just changed the two biggest levers (zero-torque head, lie). Roll the DF3i with the conventional hold above for two weeks: one 20-ball 5-footer test per week, tapped into the Putting Lab with miss directions. GATE: 2 or fewer left misses per 20 in both weeks → conventional wins, experiment over, don't add complexity to a solved problem. 3+ lefts in either week → advance to Stage 1.

sections.t: Stage 1 — the head-to-head (only if the gate trips)
sections.b: Practice-green session: alternate 10-ball blocks from 5 feet — conventional, LHL, claw, then repeat (60 balls, two blocks each). Log every block in the Decisions 10-ball tester named 'DF3i · conventional', 'DF3i · LHL', 'DF3i · claw' with miss pattern notes. Then the lag check: 5 balls from 30 feet with each grip — average leave distance matters as much as makes, because alternative grips tax distance feel first. Two separate sessions minimum before any verdict.

sections.t: Stage 2 — commit and groove
sections.b: Winner = most makes + fewest left misses across all blocks, with a lag leave you can live with. Commit for 3 full weeks before re-judging (every grip change feels bad for two weeks — that's adaptation, not information). Hybrid is legitimate: conventional for lag, claw inside 10 feet, if the blocks split that way. Film one face-on session with the winner so the early-lift verdict lands on the final setup.

sections.t: Hardware note
sections.b: The Press Pistol 2° stays — flat-front, face-square-friendly, works for all three holds. Future nudge only if hands stay noisy: an oversized SuperStroke-style grip damps wrist rotation further. Later, only if the data asks.

entries.text: Grip Experiment running — Stage 0: two weekly 20-ball tests with conventional grip. Gate: 3+ left misses advances to the grip head-to-head (plan on Home)

entries.setup: GRIP EXPERIMENT · Stage 0 block 1 · 10× 4-footers · conventional grip · DF3i · return mat
entries.finding: 8/10. Both misses died AT the hole — one beside the cup on the pull side, one hung on the near-left lip. No face-flip pattern anywhere: every start line tracked the center. The old blow-through left miss did not appear.

detail.story: First measured block of the Grip Experiment, scored frame-by-frame from ten clips. Eight drops. The scoring learned this mat's behavior on the way: made balls fall through and auto-return the full length of the mat at steady speed (which is how putt 3 — initially scored short — was corrected to a make: gravity can't roll a ball six feet back up a flat mat). The two real misses share a signature: both balls DIED at the hole. One settled an inch to the pull side, one hung on the near-left lip. That's dying-speed variance, not the old face-rotation miss — start lines were clean on all ten.
detail.compare: Versus the old baseline: the catastrophic left start line is gone. What's left is arrival-speed fine-tuning — a firmer 'back of the cup' pace would likely have converted both misses.
detail.limits: Ramped return mat punishes dying pace more than a real green (a real cup accepts soft-entry putts these lips rejected). 4-footers, not the protocol's 5 — still counts as Stage 0 evidence, but run the official 20-ball at 5 feet for the gate. Clips end at arrival, so 'vanish + full-length return' = make is the scoring rule.

test.note: 8/10 · misses: 1 pull-side at cup, 1 lip near-left · both died arriving · start lines clean

faults.why: the one open fault — film face-on with the DF3i to close it
Stage 0 block 1: 8/10 conventional, zero face-flips — misses were pace at the lip. Run the official 20-ball at 5ft for the gate

entries.finding: 8/10. Unbiased pattern read: all 10 start lines clean (no face-flip). The signal is at ARRIVAL — 2 misses + 2 lip-hangs, all on dying pace, and both misses settled on the same (pull) side. Working hypothesis: soft-pace drift, not a stroke fault — three testable causes queued for the next block.

detail.story: Scored deliberately without assuming the old left-miss theory. What the film actually shows: ten clean start lines — the face-rotation era is over on this evidence. The live signal is arrival speed: four putts arrived dying (two hung on the lip before dropping — putts 6 and 9 — and two died out). Secondary observation, flagged at low confidence: both genuine misses settled on the same side (the pull side). Two data points is a lean, not a pattern. Three candidate causes, all testable: (1) a tiny pull bias that only shows when the ball decelerates below hole-speed; (2) mat lean or seam near the cup; (3) a whisker of aim. None of them is the old face-flip — that would show in the start lines, and it doesn't.
detail.compare: Versus the diagnosis era: the catastrophic miss is gone. What's left is one notch of arrival pace and a two-putt side lean worth tracking honestly.
detail.limits: Handheld camera (it drops as each putt ends) prevented measured entry-side tracking on makes this block. NEXT BLOCK: prop the phone on the chair beside the mat — a stable frame lets the analysis measure every ball's entry point relative to the cup in fractions of an inch, makes included. Test the mat too: roll 3 balls at dead-slow speed down the center line with no stroke; if they drift the same way, cause (2) is convicted.

faults.why: the one open stroke fault — film face-on with the DF3i to close it
DF3i pace calibration: lags short + short putts firm at Hudson Hills + mat arrivals dying — the new head's power curve isn't learned yet. Lag ladder drill, twice this week

metrics.name: Path
metrics.verdict: SETTLED — straight (SBST), confirmed across 20+ clips, three putters, four sessions.

metrics.name: Tempo / load
metrics.verdict: HOLDING — measured 2.4:1 with the DF3i (0.55s/0.23s). The One-Two drill did its job; keep it as maintenance.

metrics.name: Face at impact
metrics.verdict: Square at address and start lines prove it at impact — and the DF3i now guarantees it by design.

metrics.name: Early lift
metrics.verdict: THE LAST OPEN FAULT — no angle yet has re-tested it. One face-on clip with the DF3i closes the book.

metrics.name: Start line / aim
metrics.verdict: 5/5 on the center line with the new putter. The left miss has not appeared on film since the switch.
